nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated. Generally found in small aggregations among or near mounds of branching corals such as Porites cylindrica or P. nigrescens, and around sheltered lagoon and seaward reef areas. Reported depth range 2–20 m. Often takes shelter near the spines of long-spined sea urchins, in sea-grass beds, or among rockwork during the day and forages more actively at dusk and through the night.

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied carnivore diet of mysid shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped marine flesh, krill, copepods, amphipods, and quality prepared frozen carnivore foods. Pellet and flake foods tend to be accepted once specimens are settled, though newly imported individuals often refuse dry foods initially. Multiple small feedings per day are generally preferred, and dusk/evening feeding has been reported to improve acceptance given the species' nocturnal foraging tendency. Live foods may be needed to start newly arrived specimens feeding.

summary: A small, peaceful Indo-West Pacific cardinalfish marked by ochre-orange and tan body bands, blue-edged eyes, and a black caudal-peduncle spot. Generally reef-safe and best kept as a small shoal with ample rockwork and shaded retreats.
careNotes:
The Ochrestriped Cardinalfish is a small Indo-West Pacific apogonid, typically reaching around 8–12 cm in length, with a distinctive ochre, orange, and tan barred body, large blue-rimmed eyes, and a black spot on the caudal peduncle that has been suggested to function as predator-confusion mimicry of a second eye. In nature it forms small aggregations sheltering by day among branching Porites corals, among the spines of long-spined sea urchins, or in sea-grass beds, and forages more actively at dusk and at night on zooplankton and small crustaceans.

In the aquarium it tends to do best when given ample live rock with caves and overhangs, dim shaded retreats, and tankmates of similarly peaceful disposition. It is generally reported as reef-safe and has not been documented to nip corals or sessile invertebrates; very small ornamental shrimp may occasionally be eaten as with most marine carnivores. Sources consistently describe a peaceful, shoaling temperament — small groups establish a loose hierarchy without overt aggression, in contrast to several other cardinalfishes that fight as adults.

The species is largely nocturnal in its feeding habits. Newly imported specimens tend to be transport-sensitive and may initially refuse prepared foods; live or vitamin-enriched frozen foods at dusk are generally helpful for getting individuals feeding. Once settled, the species is reported as durable provided water quality and feeding are consistent.

Cross-source notes (this entry is marked needs_review for tank size): LiveAquaria gives a minimum tank size of 55 US gallons (~208 L) for a 4.5-inch fish (~11.4 cm); Reeflex recommends 1000 L and rates it as 'only for advanced aquarists' citing transport sensitivity; Tank Facts describes it as easy and gives a smaller adult size (~7.6 cm) without a tank size; FishBase gives 12.0 cm TL (8.5 cm SL common). The 300 L value written here biases toward the larger end of the practical retail/community-aquarium range without adopting Reeflex's outlier 1000 L recommendation, on the reasoning that a small shoal of a ~12 cm reef-associated fish needs swimming room and structured rockwork but does not require a 1000 L volume. Care level was averaged across sources (easy / easy / advanced) to 'intermediate', reflecting the species' transport sensitivity on arrival. A human reviewer should sanity-check the tank-size figure for typical use cases.

RESOLVED 2026-06-03: Tank-size concern resolved by consulting Blue Zoo Aquatics (75 gal / ~284 L, care level Intermediate) as an additional retail source, giving a three-source retail spread of LiveAquaria ~208 L (Easy), Blue Zoo ~284 L (Intermediate), and Reeflex 1000 L (Advanced, outlier). FishBase provides no tank-size figure. The 300 L value sits just above both mainstream retail recommendations and is the appropriate nice-list value for a group of 5 (minGroupSize) of a ~12 cm shoaling species that requires structured rockwork — it is not derived from the Reeflex outlier. Blue Zoo's Intermediate care rating corroborates the 'intermediate' careLevel used here. No change to tank.minVolumeLiters is warranted; dataStatus advanced to 'researched'.
breedingNotes: Like other cardinalfishes, Ostorhinchus compressus is a paternal mouthbrooder with distinct pair formation during courtship and spawning. After spawning the male carries the egg mass in his buccal cavity for roughly 1–3 weeks and largely ceases feeding while brooding; a visibly swollen jaw is the most obvious sign. The species has been successfully reared in captivity per FishBase, though, as with most apogonids, larvae are pelagic and survival in community tanks tends to be low without a dedicated rearing setup.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source — gave scientific name, care level (easy), temperament (peaceful), minimum tank size (55 gal / ~208 L), reef-safe status, diet, color form, water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), and the no-aggression-when-grouped behaviour. Adult max size 4.5 in (~11.4 cm).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Apogonidae, Kurtiformes), max size 12.0 cm TL (8.5 cm SL common), depth range 2–20 m, Indo-West Pacific distribution (Malaysia to Solomon Islands, north to Ryukyu Islands, south to Great Barrier Reef), habitat (near branching Porites mounds), wild temperature 26.6–29.3°C, paternal mouthbrooding reproduction, and successfully reared in captivity. IUCN Least Concern (2009).

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (11–12 cm), temperature range (22–28°C), shoaling/swarmfish behaviour, peaceful temperament, reef-safe status, and diet (amphipods, copepods, mysis, krill, brine shrimp, frozen and flake foods). Source recommends 1000 L minimum and rates the species 'only for advanced aquarists' citing transport sensitivity during acclimation — this is the high outlier in the tank-size spread documented in careNotes.

additional.name: Tank Facts
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperament (peaceful, social, may become territorial toward conspecifics), care level (easy/hardy), diet (small crustaceans/plankton; brine shrimp, mysis), pH (7.8–8.4), SG (1.023–1.026), dKH (8–12), and reproduction (nocturnal spawner, male broods ~1 week). Adult size given as ~7.6 cm appears low relative to other sources and FishBase max 12 cm TL — treated as outlier.

fish.breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder; pair forms during courtship and spawning. Males carry egg mass in buccal cavity for roughly 1–3 weeks and largely stop feeding while brooding. Has been successfully reared in captivity per FishBase, but larval survival in community tanks tends to be low without a dedicated rearing setup.
